title: Comprehensive data exploration with Julia
text:
So… What can we expect?
In order to understand our data, we can look at each variable and try to understand their meaning and relevance to this problem. I know this is time-consuming, but it will give us the flavour of our dataset.
In order to have some discipline in our analysis, we can create an Excel spreadsheet with the following columns:

Variable - Variable name.
Type - Identification of the variables’ type. There are two possible values for this field: ‘numerical’ or ‘categorical’. By ‘numerical’ we mean variables for which the values are numbers, and by ‘categorical’ we mean variables for which the values are categories.
Segment - Identification of the variables’ segment. We can define three possible segments building, space or location. When we say ‘building’, we mean a variable that relates to the physical characteristics of the building (e.g. ‘OverallQual’). When we say ‘space’, we mean a variable that reports space properties of the house (e.g. ‘TotalBsmtSF’). Finally, when we say a ‘location’, we mean a variable that gives information about the place where the house is located (e.g. ‘Neighborhood’).
Expectation - Our expectation about the variable influence in ‘SalePrice’. We can use a categorical scale with ‘High’, ‘Medium’ and ‘Low’ as possible values.
Conclusion - Our conclusions about the importance of the variable, after we give a quick look at the data. We can keep with the same categorical scale as in ‘Expectation’.
Comments - Any general comments that occured to us.

While ‘Type’ and ‘Segment’ is just for possible future reference, the column ‘Expectation’ is important because it will help us develop a ‘sixth sense’. To fill this column, we should read the description of all the variables and, one by one, ask ourselves:

Do we think about this variable when we are buying a house? (e.g. When we think about the house of our dreams, do we care about its ‘Masonry veneer type’?).
If so, how important would this variable be? (e.g. What is the impact of having ‘Excellent’ material on the exterior instead of ‘Poor’? And of having ‘Excellent’ instead of ‘Good’?).
Is this information already described in any other variable? (e.g. If ‘LandContour’ gives the flatness of the property, do we really need to know the ‘LandSlope’?).

After this daunting exercise, we can filter the spreadsheet and look carefully to the variables with ‘High’ ‘Expectation’. Then, we can rush into some scatter plots between those variables and ‘SalePrice’, filling in the ‘Conclusion’ column which is just the correction of our expectations.
I went through this process and concluded that the following variables can play an important role in this problem:

OverallQual (which is a variable that I don’t like because I don’t know how it was computed; a funny exercise would be to predict ‘OverallQual’ using all the other variables available).
YearBuilt.
TotalBsmtSF.
GrLivArea.

I ended up with two ‘building’ variables (‘OverallQual’ and ‘YearBuilt’) and two ‘space’ variables (‘TotalBsmtSF’ and ‘GrLivArea’). This might be a little bit unexpected as it goes against the real estate mantra that all that matters is ‘location, location and location’. It is possible that this quick data examination process was a bit harsh for categorical variables. For example, I expected the ‘Neigborhood’ variable to be more relevant, but after the data examination I ended up excluding it. Maybe this is related to the use of scatter plots instead of boxplots, which are more suitable for categorical variables visualization. The way we visualize data often influences our conclusions.
However, the main point of this exercise was to think a little about our data and expectactions, so I think we achieved our goal. Now it’s time for ‘a little less conversation, a little more action please’. Let’s shake it!

title: Comprehensive data exploration with Julia
text:
Analysing SalePrice
‘SalePrice’ is the reason of our quest. It’s like when we’re going to a party. We always have a reason to be there. Usually, women are that reason. (disclaimer: adapt it to men, dancing or alcohol, according to your preferences)
Using the women analogy, let’s build a little story, the story of ‘How we met ’SalePrice’.
Everything started in our Kaggle party, when we were looking for a dance partner. After a while searching in the dance floor, we saw a girl, near the bar, using dance shoes. That’s a sign that she’s there to dance. We spend much time doing predictive modelling and participating in analytics competitions, so talking with girls is not one of our super powers. Even so, we gave it a try:
‘Hi, I’m Kaggly! And you? ’SalePrice’? What a beautiful name! You know ‘SalePrice’, could you give me some data about you? I just developed a model to calculate the probability of a successful relationship between two people. I’d like to apply it to us!’
describe(df_train, cols=:SalePrice)

Very well… It seems that your minimum price is larger than zero. Excellent! You don’t have one of those personal traits that would destroy my model! Do you have any picture that you can send me? I don’t know… like, you in the beach… or maybe a selfie in the gym?
plot(df_train, x=:SalePrice, kind="histogram")

Ah! I see you that you use seaborn makeup when you’re going out… That’s so elegant! I also see that you:

Deviate from the normal distribution
Have appreciable positive skewness
Show peakedness

This is getting interesting! ‘SalePrice’, could you give me your body measures?
using StatsBase
skw, kurt = skewness(collect(df_train.SalePrice)), kurtosis(collect(df_train.SalePrice))
println("Skewness: $skw \nKurtosis: $kurt")
This prints:
Skewness: 1.8809407460340335 
Kurtosis: 6.509812011089398
Amazing! If my love calculator is correct, our success probability is 97.834657%. I think we should meet again! Please, keep my number and give me a call if you’re free next Friday. See you in a while, crocodile!
‘SalePrice’, her buddies and her interests
It is military wisdom to choose the terrain where you will fight. As soon as ‘SalePrice’ walked away, we went to Facebook. Yes, now this is getting serious. Notice that this is not stalking. It’s just an intense research of an individual, if you know what I mean.
According to her profile, we have some common friends. Besides Chuck Norris, we both know ‘GrLivArea’ and ‘TotalBsmtSF’. Moreover, we also have common interests such as ‘OverallQual’ and ‘YearBuilt’. This looks promising!
To take the most out of our research, we will start by looking carefully at the profiles of our common friends and later we will focus on our common interests. Relationship with numerical variables
plot(scatter(df_train, x=:GrLivArea, y=:SalePrice, mode="markers"))

Hmmm… It seems that ‘SalePrice’ and ‘GrLivArea’ are really old friends, with a linear relationship.
And what about ‘TotalBsmtSF’?
plot(scatter(df_train, x=:TotalBsmtSF, y=:SalePrice, mode="markers"))

‘TotalBsmtSF’ is also a great friend of ‘SalePrice’ but this seems a much more emotional relationship! Everything is ok and suddenly, in a strong linear (exponential?) reaction, everything changes. Moreover, it’s clear that sometimes ‘TotalBsmtSF’ closes in itself and gives zero credit to ‘SalePrice’.

Relationship with categorical features
plot(df_train, x=:OverallQual, y=:SalePrice, color=:OverallQual, kind="box")

Like all the pretty girls, ‘SalePrice’ enjoys ‘OverallQual’. Note to self: consider whether McDonald’s is suitable for the first date.

title: Comprehensive data exploration with Julia
text:
In summary
Stories aside, we can conclude that: - ‘GrLivArea’ and ‘TotalBsmtSF’ seem to be linearly related with ‘SalePrice’. Both relationships are positive, which means that as one variable increases, the other also increases. In the case of ‘TotalBsmtSF’, we can see that the slope of the linear relationship is particularly high. - ‘OverallQual’ and ‘YearBuilt’ also seem to be related with ‘SalePrice’. The relationship seems to be stronger in the case of ‘OverallQual’, where the box plot shows how sales prices increase with the overall quality.
We just analysed four variables, but there are many other that we should analyse. The trick here seems to be the choice of the right features (feature selection) and not the definition of complex relationships between them (feature engineering).
That said, let’s separate the wheat from the chaff.
As an engineer, I don’t feel comfortable with this approach. All my education was about developing a disciplined mind, able to withstand the winds of subjectivity. There’s a reason for that. Try to be subjective in structural engineering and you will see physics making things fall down. It can hurt.
So, let’s overcome inertia and do a more objective analysis.
The ‘plasma soup’
‘In the very beginning there was nothing except for a plasma soup. What is known of these brief moments in time, at the start of our study of cosmology, is largely conjectural. However, science has devised some sketch of what probably happened, based on what is known about the universe today.’ (source: http://umich.edu/~gs265/bigbang.htm)
To explore the universe, we will start with some practical recipes to make sense of our ‘plasma soup’:

Correlation matrix (heatmap style).
‘SalePrice’ correlation matrix (zoomed heatmap style).
Scatter plots between the most correlated variables (move like Jagger style).

For heatmaps, I found JuliaPlots to be more userfriendly than PlotlyJS.jl
import Plots.heatmap as ht 
# alias because Plotly namescope conflicts with Plots.jl

using Statistics: cor 
# correlation matrix

avoid = names(df_train, String)

# avoiding columns with string values : raises error
df_non_str = select(df_train, Not(avoid));
co = cor(Matrix(df_non_str));

not_avoid = names(df_train, Not(avoid));

ht(co, xticks=(1:35, not_avoid), yticks=(1:35, not_avoid), 
    aspect_ratio=:equal, fill_z=co, xrotation=90, xtickfontsize=5, 
    ytickfontsize=5)

According to our crystal ball, these are the variables most correlated with ‘SalePrice’. My thoughts on this:

‘OverallQual’, ‘GrLivArea’ and ‘TotalBsmtSF’ are strongly correlated with ‘SalePrice’. Check!
‘GarageCars’ and ‘GarageArea’ are also some of the most strongly correlated variables. However, as we discussed in the last sub-point, the number of cars that fit into the garage is a consequence of the garage area. ‘GarageCars’ and ‘GarageArea’ are like twin brothers. You’ll never be able to distinguish them. Therefore, we just need one of these variables in our analysis (we can keep ‘GarageCars’ since its correlation with ‘SalePrice’ is higher).
‘TotalBsmtSF’ and ‘1stFloor’ also seem to be twin brothers. We can keep ‘TotalBsmtSF’ just to say that our first guess was right (re-read ‘So… What can we expect?’).
‘FullBath’?? Really?
‘TotRmsAbvGrd’ and ‘GrLivArea’, twin brothers again. Is this dataset from Chernobyl?
Ah… ‘YearBuilt’… It seems that ‘YearBuilt’ is slightly correlated with ‘SalePrice’. Honestly, it scares me to think about ‘YearBuilt’ because I start feeling that we should do a little bit of time-series analysis to get this right. I’ll leave this as a homework for you.

Let’s proceed to the pair plots.
Pair plots between ‘SalePrice’ and correlated variables (move like Jagger style)
corner(df_train[:, cols])

Although we already know some of the main figures, this mega scatter plot gives us a reasonable idea about variables relationships.
One of the figures we may find interesting is the one between ‘TotalBsmtSF’ and ‘GrLiveArea’. In this figure we can see the dots drawing a linear line, which almost acts like a border. It totally makes sense that the majority of the dots stay below that line. Basement areas can be equal to the above ground living area, but it is not expected a basement area bigger than the above ground living area (unless you’re trying to buy a bunker).
The plot concerning ‘SalePrice’ and ‘YearBuilt’ can also make us think. In the bottom of the ‘dots cloud’, we see what almost appears to be a shy exponential function (be creative). We can also see this same tendency in the upper limit of the ‘dots cloud’ (be even more creative). Also, notice how the set of dots regarding the last years tend to stay above this limit (I just wanted to say that prices are increasing faster now).
Pheww ! That’s it for today. Today we took a look at how cool Julia is and why can it rescue data scientists just in case Python disappears from this earth !

title: RAG Foundations: Building and Evaluating Your First QA System
text:
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has rapidly become the cornerstone of modern AI applications, enabling Large Language Models (LLMs) to reason over private, real-time data. While the basic idea is simple—find relevant information and give it to an LLM to generate an answer—the journey from a proof-of-concept to a robust, production-ready system is filled with nuance and complexity.
This guide is the first of a two-part series designed to take you from zero to hero in building advanced RAG systems. In this post, we’ll lay the groundwork by building a complete, functional RAG pipeline from scratch. We’ll take a hands-on, code-first approach, covering everything from data ingestion to the critical process of evaluation. Let’s get started.


At its heart, a RAG pipeline has two core components: a Retriever and a Generator. But before either can do their job, we need to prepare the knowledge base they will operate on. This is the data ingestion pipeline. Your RAG system is only as good as the data it has access to.


The first step is always to load and prepare your source documents. For our example, we’ll use the Weights & Biases documentation, which consists of several markdown files.

title: RAG - Zero to Hero
text:
At its heart, a RAG pipeline has two core components: a Retriever and a Generator. The retriever’s job is to find the most relevant pieces of information from a large knowledge base, and the generator’s job is to use that information to craft a human-like answer. In this section, we’ll build our very first, simple RAG pipeline from scratch.


Your RAG system is only as good as the data it has access to. The first step is always to load and prepare your source documents. For our example, we’ll use the Weights & Biases documentation, which consists of several markdown files.






Code Breakdown: Loading Documents



This Python snippet uses the pathlib library to interact with the file system.

pathlib.Path(...) creates an object representing the directory containing our documentation.
.rglob("*.md") recursively finds all files ending with .md (markdown) in that directory.
We then loop through each file path, read its content using .read_text(), and store it in a list of dictionaries. Each dictionary contains the raw content and some metadata, like the file’s original source path.



import pathlib

# Assuming the data has been downloaded to this directory
docs_dir = pathlib.Path("../data/wandb_docs")
docs_files = sorted(docs_dir.rglob("*.md"))

print(f"Number of files: {len(docs_files)}\n")

# Store the files as dictionaries with content and metadata
data = []
for file in docs_files:
    content = file.read_text()
    data.append(
        {
            "content": content,
            "metadata": {
                "source": str(file.relative_to(docs_dir)),
                # A naive token count for now
                "raw_tokens": len(content.split()),
            },
        }
    )

print("Example document structure:")
print(data[0])



LLMs have a finite context window, meaning they can only process a certain amount of text at once. Furthermore, feeding them smaller, more relevant pieces of text is more efficient and often leads to better results. This is where chunking comes in. We split our large documents into smaller, manageable chunks. A simple strategy is to split the text by a fixed number of tokens.






Code Breakdown: Simple Chunking



The split_into_chunks function implements a basic fixed-size, overlapping chunking strategy.
It first splits the entire text into a list of words (or “tokens” in this naive case).
It then iterates through the list, grabbing a chunk_size number of words at a time.
The chunk_overlap parameter makes the next chunk start a few words before the previous one ended. This can help preserve context that might otherwise be lost at the boundary of two chunks.
Finally, we apply this function to every document we loaded, creating a new, much larger list of chunked_data.


from typing import List

# These are hyperparameters of our ingestion pipeline
CHUNK_SIZE = 300
CHUNK_OVERLAP = 0

def split_into_chunks(
    text: str, chunk_size: int = CHUNK_SIZE, chunk_overlap: int = CHUNK_OVERLAP
) -> List[str]:
    """A simple function to split text into overlapping chunks."""
    tokens = text.split()
    chunks = []
    start = 0
    while start < len(tokens):
        end = start + chunk_size
        chunk = tokens[start:end]
        chunks.append(" ".join(chunk))
        start = end - chunk_overlap
    return chunks

# Let's chunk all our documents
chunked_data = []
for doc in data:
    chunks = split_into_chunks(doc["content"])
    for chunk in chunks:
        chunked_data.append(
            {
                "content": chunk,
                "metadata": {
                    "source": doc["metadata"]["source"],
                    "raw_tokens": len(chunk.split()),
                },
            }
        )

print(f"Original documents: {len(data)}")
print(f"Total chunks: {len(chunked_data)}")



Raw text often contains formatting artifacts, special characters, or other “noise” that can confuse an LLM. A crucial preprocessing step is to clean the text to make it as “pure” as possible for the model.






Code Breakdown: Text Cleaning



This function, make_text_tokenization_safe, performs a few simple cleaning steps using Python’s built-in re (regular expressions) and string libraries.
text.lower() converts everything to lowercase to ensure consistency.
re.sub(r”[^\w\s\d]“,” “, text) removes any character that is not a word character, whitespace, or digit.
text.translate(…) is an efficient way to remove all punctuation.
re.sub(r”+“,” “, text).strip() collapses multiple whitespace characters into a single space and removes leading/trailing spaces.


import re
import string

def make_text_tokenization_safe(text: str) -> str:
    """Removes special characters and normalizes whitespace."""
    # A simplified cleaning function
    text = text.lower()
    text = re.sub(r"[^\w\s\d]", " ", text)
    text = text.translate(str.maketrans("", "", string.punctuation))
    text = re.sub(r"\s+", " ", text).strip()
    return text

cleaned_data = []
for doc in chunked_data:
    cleaned_doc = doc.copy()
    cleaned_doc["cleaned_content"] = make_text_tokenization_safe(doc["content"])
    cleaned_data.append(cleaned_doc)

print("Original chunk:")
print(chunked_data[100]['content'])
print("\nCleaned chunk:")
print(cleaned_data[100]['cleaned_content'])



Now that we have clean, chunked data, we need a way to retrieve the chunks that are most relevant to a user’s query. We’ll start with a classic information retrieval technique: TF-IDF (Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency). TF-IDF vectorizes text by giving more weight to words that are frequent in a specific document but rare across all documents.






Code Breakdown: TF-IDF Retriever



This TFIDFRetriever class encapsulates the logic for TF-IDF retrieval.
index_data: This method takes our list of cleaned documents and uses scikit-learn’s TfidfVectorizer to convert them into a numerical matrix (self.index). This only needs to be done once.
search: This method takes a new user query. It first transforms the query into a TF-IDF vector using the same vectorizer. Then, it uses scipy’s cdist to compute the cosine distance between the query vector and all the document vectors in the index.
argsort()[:k] finds the indices of the k documents with the smallest distance (i.e., the most similar ones).
Finally, it formats the results, including the original text and a similarity score.


import weave
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import TfidfVectorizer
from scipy.spatial.distance import cdist

# By inheriting from weave.Model, we can track our retriever's experiments
class TFIDFRetriever(weave.Model):
    vectorizer: TfidfVectorizer = TfidfVectorizer()
    index: list = None
    data: list = None

    def index_data(self, data):
        """Builds the TF-IDF index from the documents."""
        self.data = data
        docs = [doc["cleaned_content"] for doc in data]
        self.index = self.vectorizer.fit_transform(docs)

    @weave.op()
    def search(self, query, k=5):
        """Searches the index for the most relevant documents."""
        query_vec = self.vectorizer.transform([query])
        # Using cosine distance to find the closest vectors
        cosine_distances = cdist(
            query_vec.todense(), self.index.todense(), metric="cosine"
        )[0]
        top_k_indices = cosine_distances.argsort()[:k]
        
        output = []
        for idx in top_k_indices:
            output.append(
                {
                    "source": self.data[idx]["metadata"]["source"],
                    "text": self.data[idx]["cleaned_content"],
                    "score": 1 - cosine_distances[idx],
                }
            )
        return output

    @weave.op()
    def predict(self, query: str, k: int):
        return self.search(query, k)

# Let's test it
retriever = TFIDFRetriever()
retriever.index_data(cleaned_data)
query = "How do I log metrics in my training script?"
search_results = retriever.predict(query, k=3)

for result in search_results:
    print(f"Score: {result['score']:.4f}, Source: {result['source']}")
    print(f"Text: {result['text'][:100]}...\n")



The final step is to take the user’s query and the retrieved context and generate a coherent answer. We’ll use a powerful LLM for this, accessed via an API (in this case, Cohere’s command-r model).






Code Breakdown: Response Generator



The SimpleResponseGenerator class handles the interaction with the LLM.
init: It initializes the Cohere client with an API key. It also stores the LLM model name and a prompt string.
create_messages: This method formats the input for the LLM. Most chat models expect a list of messages, each with a role (system, user, or assistant). The system prompt gives the LLM its high-level instructions.
predict: This is the main method. It takes the user query and the context from our retriever. It formats the context into the special documents parameter that Cohere’s API uses for RAG. It then calls the client.chat method and returns the generated text.


import os
import cohere

class SimpleResponseGenerator(weave.Model):
    model: str
    prompt: str
    client: cohere.Client = None

    def __init__(self, model: str, prompt: str):
        super().__init__(model=model, prompt=prompt)
        self.client = cohere.Client(os.environ.get("COHERE_API_KEY"))

    def create_messages(self, query: str):
        return [
            {"role": "system", "content": self.prompt},
            {"role": "user", "content": query},
        ]

    @weave.op()
    def predict(self, query: str, context: List[Dict[str, any]]):
        documents = [{"source": item["source"], "text": item["text"]} for item in context]
        messages = self.create_messages(query)
        response = self.client.chat(
            messages=messages,
            model=self.model,
            documents=documents,
            temperature=0.1,
        )
        return response.chat_message.message


# A simple prompt to guide the LLM
INITIAL_PROMPT = "Answer the following question about W&B. Provide a helpful and complete answer based only on the provided documents."

response_generator = SimpleResponseGenerator(model="command-r", prompt=INITIAL_PROMPT)
answer = response_generator.predict(query, search_results)
print(answer)



We can encapsulate this entire workflow into a single class. This makes our system modular and easy to run.






Code Breakdown: The Full Pipeline



The SimpleRAGPipeline class is a straightforward container that orchestrates the two main components.
It holds an instance of our retriever and our response_generator.
The predict method defines the core RAG logic: it takes a query, calls the retriever to get the context, and then passes both the query and the context to the response generator to get the final answer.


class SimpleRAGPipeline(weave.Model):
    retriever: TFIDFRetriever
    response_generator: SimpleResponseGenerator
    top_k: int = 5

    @weave.op()
    def predict(self, query: str):
        context = self.retriever.predict(query, self.top_k)
        return self.response_generator.predict(query, context)

# Initialize and run the full pipeline
rag_pipeline = SimpleRAGPipeline(
    retriever=retriever, response_generator=response_generator, top_k=5
)
response = rag_pipeline.predict("How do I get get started with wandb?")
print(response)
And there you have it! A complete, albeit simple, RAG pipeline. In the following sections, we will systematically upgrade each component to build a truly advanced system.
If You Can’t Measure It, You Can’t Improve It Building a RAG system is an iterative science. To improve our pipeline, we need a robust way to evaluate its performance. We’ll break down evaluation into two parts: Retrieval Evaluation and Response Evaluation.
Evaluating the Retriever The goal of the retriever is to find the most relevant documents. We can measure this using classic information retrieval metrics. To do this, we need an evaluation dataset with questions and corresponding “ground truth” relevant documents.
Hit Rate: Did the retriever find any relevant document?
Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR): How high up in the ranking was the first relevant document?
Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (NDCG): A more nuanced metric that scores the overall quality of the ranking, giving more weight to highly relevant documents ranked higher.






Code Breakdown: Retrieval Metrics



These functions calculate two common retrieval metrics.
compute_hit_rate: It checks if there’s any overlap between the set of retrieved document sources and the set of known relevant sources. It returns 1.0 for a “hit” and 0.0 for a “miss”.
compute_mrr: It finds the rank of the first relevant document in the retrieved list. The score is the reciprocal of that rank (e.g., rank 1 = 1.0, rank 2 = 0.5, rank 3 = 0.33). If no relevant document is found, the score is 0.
These metrics can be run across an entire evaluation dataset to get an average score for the retriever’s performance.


from typing import Any, Dict, List
import numpy as np

@weave.op
def compute_hit_rate(output: List[Dict[str, Any]], contexts: List[Dict[str, Any]]) -> float:
    retrieved_sources = {doc["source"] for doc in output}
    relevant_sources = {ctx["source"] for ctx in contexts if ctx.get("relevance", 1) > 0}
    return 1.0 if retrieved_sources.intersection(relevant_sources) else 0.0

@weave.op
def compute_mrr(output: List[Dict[str, Any]], contexts: List[Dict[str, Any]]) -> float:
    relevant_sources = {ctx["source"] for ctx in contexts if ctx.get("relevance", 1) > 0}
    for i, doc in enumerate(output):
        if doc["source"] in relevant_sources:
            return 1.0 / (i + 1)
    return 0.0

# Assume `eval_dataset` is a list of {"question": "...", "contexts": [...]}
# We can use weave.Evaluation to run these metrics over our dataset
# eval_dataset = ... (loaded from a file)
# retrieval_evaluation = weave.Evaluation(
#     dataset=eval_dataset,
#     scorers=[compute_hit_rate, compute_mrr]
# )
# scores = asyncio.run(retrieval_evaluation.evaluate(retriever))



Evaluating the generated response is more complex. We care about correctness, relevance, and faithfulness to the source documents.
Traditional NLP Metrics: Metrics like ROUGE and BLEU compare the generated text to a “golden” reference answer by looking at overlapping words and phrases.
LLM as a Judge: A powerful approach is to use another, more powerful LLM (like GPT-4 or Cohere’s Command R+) to “judge” the quality of the generated answer based on a set of criteria.






Code Breakdown: LLM as a Judge



This function outlines the “LLM as a Judge” pattern.
It constructs a detailed prompt for a powerful “judge” LLM.
This prompt includes the original question, the ideal reference_answer, and the generated_answer from our RAG system.
It asks the judge LLM to provide a structured evaluation (e.g., a numerical score and a text reason) in a specific format like JSON.
The function would then make an API call to this judge LLM and parse its response. This automates the process of getting a qualitative score for our generated answers.


# This is a simplified example of the logic
# The full implementation involves careful prompt engineering
@weave.op
async def llm_correctness_scorer(output: str, question: str, answer: str) -> dict:
    # `prompt` would contain instructions for the LLM judge
    # to score the `output` against the `answer` for the given `question`
    # and return a score and a reason in JSON format.
    prompt = f"""
    Evaluate the following generated answer for correctness.
    Question: {question}
    Reference Answer: {answer}
    Generated Answer: {output}
    
    Provide a score from 0 (incorrect) to 2 (perfectly correct) and a reason.
    Return JSON with keys "score" and "reason".
    """

With a solid evaluation framework in place, we can now make targeted improvements and measure their impact.



“Garbage in, garbage out” is especially true for RAG. Let’s level up our data ingestion pipeline.
Accurate Tokenization Our initial word count was a rough estimate. Different models have different tokenizers. Using the model’s actual tokenizer gives us a precise token count, which is critical for managing chunk sizes and context windows.






Code Breakdown: Accurate Token Counting



This code replaces our simple text.split() with a call to the official Cohere tokenizer. The co.tokenize() method breaks the text down into the exact same sub-word units that the command-r model uses, giving us a precise token count. This is essential for ensuring our chunks don’t exceed the model’s context limit.

title: Introducing Falafel: AI Video Generation in DaVinci Resolve
text:
I’m super excited to announce the launch of Falafel, a new tool designed to bridge the gap between powerful AI video generation and professional video editing.
If you’ve ever wanted to generate stunning video clips from an image using diffusion models and bring them directly into your DaVinci Resolve timeline without a cumbersome export/import process, Falafel is for you.

What is Falafel?
Falafel is a free, open-source desktop application that acts as a bridge between your DaVinci Resolve media pool and the powerful models available on Fal.ai. It streamlines the creative process, letting you focus on editing, not file management.
The name ‘Falafel’ is a fun nod to its core—it’s a wrapper for Fal AI. The project is built with Electron and the source code is available for everyone on GitHub.


How It Works
Getting started is simple. The entire workflow is designed to keep you in your creative zone.

Install & Setup Download and install the Falafel desktop app. On the first launch, you’ll be prompted to sign in with Google to get started.
Connect to DaVinci Resolve Inside Resolve, navigate to the menu: Workspace -> Scripts -> Falafel. This action starts a local server that allows the two applications to communicate.
Select Your Source Image In the DaVinci Resolve Media Pool, simply click on the image you want to use as your source.
Generate in Falafel Switch over to the Falafel app.

Click the Select Image from Media Pool button. Your chosen image will appear in the app.
Enter your Fal AI API Key.
Write a descriptive prompt for the video you want to create.
Click Generate Video and let the AI work its magic. 

Import to Resolve Once the generation is complete, a new button, Import to Media Pool, will appear in Falafel. Clicking this will send the newly created video file directly into your DaVinci Resolve Media Pool, ready to be dropped into your timeline.




Key Features

Seamless Integration: A direct link between your Resolve project and AI models. No more saving, exporting, finding, and importing files.
Privacy-Focused: Your API keys are yours alone and are not stored or transmitted to any third-party server besides Fal.ai.
Open Source: Built for the community, by the community. You can inspect the code, contribute, and suggest features on GitHub.
Free to Use: Falafel is completely free. You only need an API key from Fal.ai for the generation service itself.



Get Started Now
Ready to supercharge your editing workflow? You can download the latest version of Falafel and find the source code on the official GitHub repository.
Download Falafel


The Road Ahead
This is just the beginning for Falafel. Future plans include support for more models, batch processing, and even more configuration options directly within the app.
I built Falafel to solve a problem I had, and I hope it helps you too. I welcome any feedback, bug reports, and contributions. Please feel free to open an issue or pull request on GitHub.
Thank you for checking it out!
